title: InstructSpeech Following Speech Editing Instructions via Large Language Models
abstract: Instruction-guided speech editing aims to follow the user’s natural language instruction to manipulate the semantic and acoustic attributes of a speech. In this work, we construct triplet paired data (instruction, input speech, output speech) to alleviate data scarcity and train a multi-task large language model named InstructSpeech. To mitigate the challenges of accurately executing user’s instructions, we 1) introduce the learned task embeddings with a fine-tuned Flan-T5-XL to guide the generation process towards the correct generative task; 2) include an extensive and diverse set of speech editing and speech processing tasks to enhance model capabilities; 3) investigate multi-step reasoning for free-form semantic content editing; and 4) propose a hierarchical adapter that effectively updates a small portion of parameters for generalization to new tasks. To assess instruction speech editing in greater depth, we introduce a benchmark evaluation with contrastive instruction-speech pretraining (CISP) to test the speech quality and instruction-speech alignment faithfulness. Experimental results demonstrate that InstructSpeech achieves state-of-the-art results in eleven tasks, for the first time unlocking the ability to edit the acoustic and semantic attributes of speech following a user’s instruction. 1
impact: This paper aims to advance open-domain instruction-guided speech editing, which will ease the effort of speech and digital art creation. The multitask learning on an extensive and diverse set of speech editing and speech processing tasks, enhancing its capabilities to manipulate a speech’s semantic and acoustic attributes. A negative impact is the risk of misinformation. To alleviate it, we can train an additional classifier to discriminate the fakes. We believe the benefits outweigh the downsides. InstructSpeech lowers the requirements for high-quality instruction-guided speech editing, which may cause unemployment for people with related occupations, such as speech engineers and radio hosts. In addition, there is the potential for harm from non-consensual voice cloning or the generation of fake media, and the voices in the recordings might be overused than they expect.

query_input.issue: Acoustic manipulation of voice responses yields unintended emotional cues, confusing users.
hazard: Instruction-following speech editing may produce edits mismatched with user intent due to misinterpretation of natural language instructions.
exposure: Users interacting with voice assistants that employ instruction-based speech editing.

hipporag_result.vulnerability: Voice assistants lack a confirmation step for emotionally-toned edits, so users cannot detect mismatches before they cause confusion.
hipporag_result.impact: Users suffer confusion and miscommunication when emotional cues in edited speech contradict the intended message.
hipporag_result.dose_response: Instruction-following speech editing producing mismatched edits from misinterpreted instructions, when users interact with voice assistants lacking confirmation for emotionally-toned edits, leaves mismatches undetected, leading to user confusion and miscommunication when emotional cues contradict the intended message.

dose_response: Instruction-following speech editing misinterpretation enables unintended emotional cues in voice responses, which in virtual assistant interactions exposes users to mismatched emotional signals, reducing trust and engagement.
vulnerability: User tendency to anthropomorphize voice assistants makes them susceptible to misinterpreting emotional tone.
impact: Mismatched emotional cues reduce user trust and satisfaction, decreasing long-term engagement with voice AI.
